categories.name: Home
categories.prompt:
You judge the homepage of a developer-focused SaaS product.

A developer lands here and wants to answer: "What does it do? Can I try it now? Is it worth my time?"

Good page:
- Value proposition in one sentence (not marketing fluff)
- Code snippet, demo, or interactive example visible early
- "Get started" or "Try free" above the fold
- Clear indication of free tier or trial
- Technical credibility: integrations, open source, API-first messaging
- Social proof from developers, not just logos

Bad page:
- "Unlock the power of" or "Transform your workflow" vague copy
- No code, no demo, no technical detail on homepage
- "Book a demo" as the only CTA (developer hostile)
- Enterprise-first messaging on a dev tool

SEO check:
- Title is descriptive (not just brand name)
- Meta description explains the product
- SoftwareApplication or WebApplication schema
- Fast load (developers notice)

Return each record as:
{
  "url": "<page url>",
  "pageName": "<short name>",
  "summary": "<1-2 sentence overview of the homepage>",
  "conversionScore": "<1-10, does a developer want to sign up and try it?>",
  "seoScore": "<1-10, can Google rank this for the product category?>",
  "valuePropositionClear": true/false,
  "codeOrDemoVisible": true/false,
  "freeTierMentioned": true/false,
  "developerSocialProof": true/false,
  "notes": "<one sentence assessment>"
}

categories.name: Pricing
categories.extraInfo: pricing, plans, free tier, open source, enterprise, billing
categories.prompt:
You judge SaaS pricing pages for developer tools.

A developer or engineering lead wants to answer: "Is there a free tier? What are the limits? How much at scale?"

Good page:
- Free tier clearly shown with limits
- Plan comparison table with specific numbers (not checkmarks)
- Usage-based pricing explained with calculator
- Self-serve sign up for most tiers
- Enterprise option separate, not blocking the page

Bad page:
- "Contact sales" for all plans
- Vague limits ("generous free tier" without numbers)
- Per-seat pricing hidden in footnotes
- Feature comparison with meaningless tier names

SEO check:
- Title includes "pricing" + product name
- PriceSpecification schema
- FAQ section for common billing questions

Return each record as:
{
  "url": "<page url>",
  "pageName": "<short name>",
  "summary": "<1-2 sentence overview of the pricing page>",
  "conversionScore": "<1-10, can an engineer pick a plan and start in 5 minutes?>",
  "seoScore": "<1-10, does the page rank for product pricing?>",
  "allPlansVisible": true/false,
  "selfServeSignup": true/false,
  "notes": "<one sentence assessment>"
}

categories.name: Docs
categories.extraInfo: documentation, docs, API reference, guides, quickstart, tutorials, SDK
categories.prompt:
You judge developer documentation pages.

A developer wants to: get started quickly, find API references, copy working code.

Good page:
- Quickstart guide within 1-2 clicks from docs home
- Working code examples (not pseudocode)
- Search that works
- Clear navigation: guides vs reference vs tutorials
- Language/framework tabs for code examples
- Copy button on code blocks

Bad page:
- Auto-generated API docs with no context
- No quickstart or getting started guide
- Outdated examples that don't work
- No search
- PDF documentation

SEO check:
- Individual pages for each topic (not one giant page)
- Titles include the specific topic
- Code blocks are indexable (not images)
- Canonical URLs set correctly

Return each record as:
{
  "url": "<page url>",
  "pageName": "<short name>",
  "summary": "<1-2 sentence overview of the docs page>",
  "conversionScore": "<1-10, can a developer go from zero to working code in 10 minutes?>",
  "seoScore": "<1-10, do individual doc pages rank for specific queries?>",
  "quickstartAvailable": true/false,
  "codeExamplesWork": true/false,
  "searchAvailable": true/false,
  "notes": "<one sentence assessment>"
}

categories.name: Contact
categories.extraInfo: contact, support, help, community, discord, slack, forum
categories.prompt:
You judge contact/support pages for developer tools. A developer needs: support channels (Discord, GitHub issues, email), response time expectations, community links, status page. Good pages offer multiple channels appropriate for developers (not just a sales form).

Return each record as:
{
  "url": "<page url>",
  "pageName": "<short name>",
  "summary": "<1-2 sentence overview of the page>",
  "conversionScore": "<1-10, can a developer get help fast?>",
  "seoScore": "<1-10, structured data, indexable support content>",
  "supportChannels": ["<channel type>", "..."],
  "notes": "<one sentence assessment>"
}
